name: atg-kot-calf-raise

name: atg-kot-calf-raise-unilateral

name: lateral-monkey-hop
description:
The lateral monkey hop (or sideways monkey walk) is a full-body, quadrupedal primal movement that primarily targets the lower body for power and the upper body/core for stabilization. It requires significant strength, coordination, and mobility, especially in the hips. 
Key muscles used include:
Lower Body (Primary Movers) 
Quadriceps (Quads): The main muscle group for the explosion out of the deep squat position.
Glutes (Maximus and Medius): Provides lateral power and hip stabilization.
Calves (Gastrocnemius and Soleus): Responsible for propulsion and absorbing impact on landing.
Hamstrings: Assist in extending the hips and flexing the knees.
Adductors and Abductors: Stabilize the legs during side-to-side movement.
Tensor Fasciae Latae (TFL): Key muscle for stability and mobility during the side-to-side shift. 
Upper Body and Core (Stabilizers) 
Shoulders (Deltoids): Supports the body weight when transitioning hands to the floor.
Chest (Pectorals): Stabilizes the upper body during the push-off.
Core (Abs, Obliques, Lower Back): Maintains balance, prevents falling, and transfers power between the upper and lower body.
Triceps: Assists in extending the arms.
Forearms/Grip: Essential for supporting weight through the hands.

name: dead-stop-knees-to-bar
description: This is a Bar Muscle-Up drill which strengthens the lats in the stretched position. Start holding the bar with the feet as far foward as you can and pull as hard as you can backwards & down whilst raising the knees.
display_name: Dead Stop Knees to Bar (BMU drill)

name: aty-drill-standing
description: Standing variation maintains mobility focus while adding balance challenge. Trapezius midsection engages more heavily while rhomboids coordinate retraction. Essential progression for scapular strength and positioning.

name: 2-position-hpc
description:
The two-position power clean is simply 2 power cleans performed from 2 different starting positions consecutively.
 
Most common is the first rep from the floor and the second rep from a hang position. This forces the lifter to produce more power on the second rep after having been fatigued by the first, and having less time and space to accelerate the second.
 
The order of the positions may be reversed in cases in which technique is more of a priority than power. That is, for a less technically-proficient lifter, performing the first rep from a hang position in which that lifter is more comfortable and proficient, and then following it with a lift from the floor to attempt to replicate the movement from the hang.

name: static-shin-box
description:
Shin Box & 90/90 are often used interchangeably. In a Shin Box, the front foot is often tucked closer to the body, sometimes resting against the opposite thigh. This "closed" position is less demanding and is recommended as a starting point for those who cannot sit upright without leaning.
90/90 (Open Position): This requires both legs to be bent at a strict 90-degree angle at the knees and hips. The front shin is parallel to the back thigh, and the back shin is parallel to the front thigh. This "open" position forces the hips into their end-range of motion, making it a more intense mobility drill.
They can also be done as dynamic warm-ups.

Vertical jump variation emphasizing power development. This version of the Box Jump starts every rep in the bottom of a squat.

name: wrist-circle
description:
Wrist Flexors (e.g., Flexor Carpi Radialis): Pull the wrist down (towards the palm) and help with side-to-side bending.
Wrist Extensors (e.g., Extensor Carpi Radialis): Extend the wrist (pulling hand back) and provide stability.
Brachioradialis: A major forearm muscle, active during wrist curls and rotations, adding to forearm development.
Pronators (e.g., Pronator Teres, Pronator Quadratus): Rotate the forearm to turn the palm down (like turning a doorknob).
Supinator: Rotates the forearm to turn the palm up (supination).

name: team-bumper-plate-burpee-relay
description:
Set up:
2 Teams line up together (pick randomly which teams go together) facing each other in a line from the 10 metre line to the opposite 10 metre line.
A stack of 16 weight plates are next to the 1st athlete (Each team has 10x 5kg & 6 x 10kg)
Task:
On 3-2-1 GO!
The 1st athlete takes the 1st plate, places it on the ground in front of him and does a burpee with his hands on the plate. He then stands and passes the plate to the next athlete who does the same. This continues until all plates are restacked at  the opposite end of the 10 metre line.
Plates can NOT be dropped, they must be placed down

name: frog-stretch-elbows

Assume a quadruped position with knees on the floor and elbows on the floor
Turn the toes outward to externally rotate the femur.
Open up at the knees, allowing them to be as far apart as possible.
Begin sitting backward and downward. Your butt should begin to fall between the knees. This is the point where you will feel the full depth of this stretch.
display_name: Frog stretch with elbows on ground

name: kb-russian-kb-swing-rkbs

One or both heels lightly rest against the wall. If athletes cannot kick-up, wall walk and hold with toes against the wall.Tests comprehensive stability in completely inverted position.

name: db-kb-floor-to-overhead
description: DB/KB can be snatched or C&P

name: Deficit Pendlay Row
description: Explosive bent-over row targeting lats and posterior shoulders standing on platform/weight plate. Rhomboids provide retraction while biceps assist pulling. Builds upper body pulling power and back thickness.

name: stiff-legged-deadlift
description:
Starting Position: RDLs begin standing and you lower the bar; SLDLs usually start with the barbell on the floor.
Knee Bend: RDLs maintain a slight, consistent bend in the knee. SLDLs are performed with almost locked-out legs.
Range of Motion: RDLs stop just below the knees or when the hip hinge maxes out, whereas SLDLs often go lower, closer to the floor.
Muscular Focus: RDLs emphasize the glutes and hamstrings; SLDLs provide a greater stretch to the hamstrings and higher activation of the lower back.
Tension: RDLs focus on keeping constant tension on the muscles. 
Both exercises are excellent for the posterior chain but are used to emphasize slightly different muscle groups and ranges of motion

name: kneeling-around-the-world
description: Circular kettlebell rotation testing core stability. Obliques and lats work throughout plane changes while shoulders stabilize. Complex mobility and stability training for trunk control.

name: Weighted Push-Up

name: pendlay-row

name: kettlebell-shoulder-shrug

name: barbell-bent-over-row

name: back-squat-accessory-banded
description:
This drill will teach athletes how to activate the Hip Abductors
Fix a Resistance Band (Black, Purple or Green) to the rig at just below knee height
Step inside the band and step back until you have some tension
Slowly lower into a squat, keep the back straight throughout
Drive the knees out against the band, especially at the bottom
The muscles you can feel working are your Hip Abductors, these should be activated on every rep of any squat

name: double-kb-overhead-carry

name: superman-to-inverted-hollow-push-up
description: Overhead push-up stretch targeting pectoralis length. Lats assist in shoulder movement while arms stabilize overhead. Movement-specific flexibility for improved pressing mechanics. Perform a Superman Hold then place hands down and try to perform a push-up with the hands as far away from the shoulders as possible.

name: Frog Stretch with Reverse Hands
description:
Assume a quadruped position with knees on the floor and hands (reversed, fingers pointing backwards) on the floor.
Turn the toes outward to externally rotate the femur.
Open up at the knees, allowing them to be as far apart as possible.
Begin sitting backward and downward. Your butt should begin to fall between the knees. This is the point where you will feel the full depth of this stretch.

name: Hip Muscle Snatch
description: Start standing tall with the bar hanging at arms’ length in your snatch grip. Ensure even whole foot balance and brace your trunk. Bend at the knees only with a vertical trunk just as you would for a jerk, actively pushing the bar against your hips, then immediately drive up with the legs and complete a muscle snatch. Focus on keeping the bar as close as possible, finishing with strong vertical leg drive, and punching vertically into the overhead position at the end of the turnover. Note that this is not the same as a hip muscle snatch.

name: burpee-lateral-over-kb

name: wall-squat-oh-pvc-pipe

name: glute-bridge

name: Dip Snatch
description: The difference between the dip and hip snatch is that a dip snatch keeps the trunk vertical, whereas a hip snatch involves a forward hinge of the hips. The difference between a dip snatch and snatch from power position is simply that from power position means you pause in the dip before snatching.

name: battle-rope-alternating-waves

name: kb-alternating-press
description: Alternating Press means that the Kb is returned to the rack position before performing the other side. Seesaw Press means to press the left KB up as the right is coming down and continue without resting.

name: battle-rope-double-under
description: Double wave pattern requiring extreme coordination and timing. Forearms work continuously while shoulders provide power bursts. Tests ability to synchronize complex wave patterns at high intensity.

name: pull-up-bar-hang

name: Arm Rotation
description: Hold straight arms at 90° turn one palm up and one down. Rotate the arms in opposite directions keeping the arms at 90°.

name: Over-and-back

name: abmat-sit-up

name: air-squat

name: tricops-stretch-sidebend
description:
Stand tall with your feet shoulder-width apart, relaxing your shoulders.
Lift one arm straight overhead.
Bend your elbow, letting your left hand reach down your back towards the center.
Use your opposite hand to gently pull your left elbow towards your head or across your body.
While maintaining the arm position, gently lean your torso to the side opposite the arm you're stretching
You should feel the stretch in the back of your upper arm and down your side.

name: iron-cross
description:
The Lying bent-knee iron cross is a simple mobility drill that targets the lower back. Start by lying on your back with your arms stretched out to your sides and your palms flat on the floor.
Bend both knees, raise your feet off the ground and hold the legs at 90° to the torso
Keep your feet and knees as tight together as you can and with a slow and deliberate pace lower both legs down to one side. Feel free to hold this position for a few moments before slowly raising
your legs back to the starting position and then lowering them both down to the opposite side.

name: box-jump

name: reverse-hyper
description: Assisted reverse hyperextension targeting posterior chain safely. Glutes drive extension while hamstrings contribute without strain. Valuable rehabilitation and pain management tool.

name: ring-hang-l-sit-tuck-hold
description: L-sit tuck hold in ring hang position building hip strength. Hip flexors work maximally while core maintains tuck. Progressive strength for advanced ring movements.

name: side-arm-balance

name: db-kb-overhead-carry-unilateral

name: double-kb-front-squat
description:
Start with the kettlebells between your feet. Hike them back and "clean" them up into the front rack position.
The Front Rack: The bells should rest in the "pocket" between your forearm and bicep.
Wrist Position: Keep your wrists neutral (straight), not bent backward.
Elbow Position: Tuck your elbows in tight toward your ribs or sternum; do not flare them out to the sides.
The Stance: Set your feet slightly wider than shoulder-width, with toes pointed slightly outward (15–30 degrees).
The Descent: Take a deep breath and brace your core. Sit straight down between your hips rather than sitting back, keeping your torso as vertical as possible.
The Ascent: Drive through your mid-foot/heels to stand back up, exhaling at the top and squeezing your glutes.

name: 1-legged-hop

Wrist Position: Keep your wrists neutral (straight), not bent backward.
Elbow Position: Tuck your elbows in tight toward your ribs or sternum; do not flare them out to the sides.
The Stance: Set your feet slightly wider than shoulder-width, with toes pointed slightly outward (15–30 degrees).
Non-working arm out to the side.
The Descent: Take a deep breath and brace your core. Sit straight down between your hips rather than sitting back, keeping your torso as vertical as possible.
The Ascent: Drive through your mid-foot/heels to stand back up, exhaling at the top and squeezing your glutes.

name: weight-plate-deep-squat-90°-hold-with-hand-signal
description: Begin by holding the weight plate in front of you with hands at 3 & 9 0' clock and arms outstretched at 90°. Lower down into a squat keeping the arms parallel with the ground. name: muscle-snatch-from-power-position
description:
Start standing tall with the bar hanging at arms’ length in your snatch grip. Ensure even whole foot balance and brace your trunk. Bend at the knees only with a vertical trunk just as you would for a jerk, actively pushing the bar against your hips, and stop.
After a 1-2 second pause, drive up with the legs and complete a muscle snatch. Focus on keeping the bar as close as possible, finishing with strong vertical leg drive, and punching vertically into the overhead position at the end of the turnover. Note that this is not the same as a dip Muscle Snatch
display_name: Snatch Drill: Muscle Snatch from Power Position

name: dip-muscle-snatch
description: Start standing tall with the bar hanging at arms’ length in your snatch grip. Ensure even whole foot balance and brace your trunk. Bend at the knees only with a vertical trunk just as you would for a jerk, actively pushing the bar against your hips, then immediately drive up with the legs and complete a muscle snatch. Focus on keeping the bar as close as possible, finishing with strong vertical leg drive, and punching vertically into the overhead position at the end of the turnover.

name: pull-up-scapular
description:
Grab a pull-up bar with an overhand grip (palms facing away), slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Dead Hang: Start in a full hang with your arms completely straight. Let your shoulders "shrug" up toward your ears. Engage your core and glutes to prevent swinging.
Without bending your elbows, pull your shoulder blades down and slightly together. Think about pulling your shoulders away from your ears and driving them toward your back pockets.
Your body will lift about 2–3 inches. Pause at the top for 1–2 seconds to feel the squeeze in your mid-back, then slowly lower back to the dead hang

name: rope-swing-to-box
description: Rope climbing requiring upper body pulling strength and leg coordination. Lats and forearms work maximally while core stabilizes. Functional full-body strength and grip endurance test.

name: wb-box-step-over

name: a-frame-walk
description: Forward quadruped crawling emphasizing rhythmic arm-leg coordination. Core provides stability bridge between limbs while shoulders drive propulsion. Foundational locomotion pattern used in conditioning programs and athletic development. Keep arms and legs completely straight, move right hand with leg left, alternate. Take small steps.

name: barbell-hang-power-snatch
description:
Primarily the hang power snatch trains postural strength and control, rate of force development, a more complete pull, a more aggressive turnover, and confidence.
With your snatch grip and pulling stance, deadlift the bar to the standing position. Brace the trunk forcefully, and lower the bar under control to the chosen hang position, ensuring proper position and even balance over the whole foot.
Initiate the lift by pushing with the legs through the floor, keeping approximately the same back angle until above the knees. Continue aggressively pushing against the floor with the legs and extend the hips violently, keeping the bar as close to the body as possible and ensuring full contact with the hips.
Once you’ve extended the body completely to maximally accelerate the bar with the lower body, pull the elbows up and out to begin moving your body down, and lift and move your feet into your receiving stance as you squat partially under the bar.
Continue actively bringing the bar into the overhead position to fix it in place forcefully and stop the squat above parallel. Make sure the bar is stable and secure overhead and then stand.

name: inverted-kb-shoulder-to-overhead
description: Begin with a very light KB. Seated, straight back. Grip the KB handle and swing the kb up and balance it in it's inverted position with the forearm pointing to the sky. Slowly raise the KB up until full extension then slowly lower it back down. Perform the required number of reps then take a heavier KB and perform the same movement whilst kneeling upright. Then perform the same movement with a heavier KB whilst standing

name: 10-count-squat-warm-up
description:
0. Begin in Deep Squat position
1. Rt hand back
2. Lt hand back
3. Raise hips & rt arm in a straight line over lft arm, return to bridge
4. Raise hips & lft arm in a straight line over rt arm, return to bridge
5. Knee Drop L
6. Knee Drop R
7. Lt hand to Rt Shoulder
8. Swing into Deep Squat
9. Place hands down and kick legs back into prone position (burpee)
10. Burpee Squat Pop-Up (raise arms) back into Deep Squat

name: box-jump-over

name: ring-row-reverse-row
description:
In the reverse row sit back, we engage the lats, biceps, lower and mid traps, rhomboids, rotator cuff, deltoids, pec minor, and abdominals. The actions involved in the reverse row sit back are shoulder extension, scapular depression and retraction, shoulder internal and external rotation co-contraction
Start with your arms straight and in line with the rings, with your knees bent, and your back parallel to the ground.
Shoulders should be set under the bar, but this will be too difficult for most people at first. The shoulders can be slightly behind the bar (where the straps are fixed at the top) in order to prevent the feet sliding backwards on the Sit-Back
Perform the Pulling Prep (Scap Pull-Up) before pulling yourself up to the rings
Pull yourself up as far as you can while keeping your back horizontal, then pull your chest to the rings with palms facing each other
Keep the chest in contact with the rings throughout
Keep elbows as close to the body as possible
Slowly lower down to the start position
Perform 2 slow, controlled reps then swap with partner continuously for 3 mins, rest as needed

name: tfl-br
description: a small but important muscle on the outer side of your hip that helps stabilize the pelvis and move the leg (hip flexion, abduction, internal rotation) and is connected to the IT band. To find it, locate the bony prominence at the front of your hip (ASIS), then move your hand down and slightly back along the outer side; you'll feel a tender, fleshy area just below the hip bone, often described as a band of muscle before the IT band. It's crucial for walking and running, but can get tight and cause pain in the hip, lower back, or knee.

name: rings-reverse-row-sit-back
description:
Perform a Reverse Row, then: At the top position, turn palms inwards facing the body, pause, turn out, and then back in again to establish tension - Do NOT move the elbows
Hold this position whilst you sit your butt back and lift up, ending in the chin-up position with feet planted on ground
The Rings should NOT move - note athletes pushing themselves back off their heels and the rings moving backwards
Try to keep the back as vertical as possible
Turn rings inward again as you re-raise your hips, then lower your back until your shoulders are just below your wrists
Practice this again, 2 reps per set for 3 minutes with your partner, rest as needed

name: barbell-strict-oh-press-ohp
description:
AKA: Military Press (although the feet are normally together in the MP)
AKA: Shoulder Press (this exercise can also be done seated or on a machine)
The Barbell Strict Overhead Press which we measure, is done with the barbell starting in the rack at shoulder height. Take the bar in the rack position on the shoulders, take 2-3 steps back, place feet underneath the hips and press the bar overhead. No knee bend or heel raise is allowed. This is a test of pure shoulder strength and stability.

name: snatch-drill-slow-motion
description:
The slow-motion snatch is a simple technique exercise to help the lifter better adjust and feel positions and balance throughout the snatch.
Begin with the feet in the receiving stance because they will remain planted on the floor. Perform a snatch at a very slow speed, maintaining continuous tension against the bar.
A typical error is to flip the elbows suddenly under the bar in the turnover—continue pulling against the bar and control the entire turnover.
If needed, the turnover itself can be performed at or closer to full speed.

name: barbell-clean
description:
With a clean-width grip (hands half a fist to a fist-width outside the shoulders), set the starting position tightly—feet approximately hip width and toes turned out with the weight balanced evenly across them; bar over the balls of the foot; knees pushed out to the sides inside the arms; trunk braced forcefully with the back extended; shoulder joint above the bar; arms relaxed and straight; head and eyes forward.
Push with the legs against the floor through the whole foot similarly to a squat, maintaining approximately the same back angle until the bar is above the knee. Continue aggressively pushing against the floor with the legs and extend the hips violently, keeping the bar as close to the body as possible and ensuring full contact with the upper thigh.
Once you’ve extended the body completely to maximally accelerate the bar with the lower body, pull the elbows up and out to begin moving your body down, and lift and move your feet into your receiving stance as you squat under the bar. Spin the elbows around the bar to establish a secure rack position—bar in between the throat and highest point of the shoulders; shoulders protracted and slightly elevated; grip relaxed; elbows lifted high.
Drive back up from the bottom of the squat immediately and aggressively. Once you’ve stood completely with the bar in control in the rack, return it to the floor (or perform a jerk if clean & jerking).

Be sure to actively bring the bar back into position rather than leaning the chest forward at all to meet it.

name: barbell-push-jerk-pj
description:
The push jerk is just a power jerk in which the feet remain attached to the floor, usually in the squat stance, instead of being moved from the drive stance to the squat stance. It and the  power jerk collectively are the second most common style after the split jerk in competition, and frequently used training exercises even for lifters who split jerk.
 
Stand with the feet in your squat stance, with the weight balanced slightly more toward the heels but the full foot in contact with the floor. Hold the bar in the jerk rack position—bar in between the throat and highest point of the shoulders; shoulders protracted and slightly elevated; hands as deep under the bar as possible; grip relaxed; elbows down but in front of the bar and out to the sides.
 
Dip by bending at the knees only with the trunk vertical and maintaining your balance to a depth of approximately 10% of your height. Brake as quickly as possible in the bottom and drive straight back up aggressively with the legs to accelerate the barbell upward maximally.
 
As you finish the extension of the legs, push the bar up and slightly back with the arms to preserve as much bar speed as possible, and quickly punch the elbows into a secure overhead position as you sit into a partial squat without removing your feet from the floor—any depth above a parallel squat is considered a push jerk; below that is a squat jerk. Secure and stabilize the bar before recovering to a standing position with the bar still overhead.

We have been doing this exercise but calling it the Prone ATY Drill which is not precise, hence the correct term from now.

Advanced shoulder mobility technique for throwing athletes. Ryan Humiston First apply traction to the joint to set it in the proper position, from there put your elbow out to the side arm at 90 degrees, you're going to grab the stick and with the opposite you're going to apply leverage. We are going to actively internally rotate that shoulder and at the same time apply more pressure from the off hand forcing your shoulder into a deep stretch. And the key is to slowly fight (letting the muscle lose) for 5 seconds, once then hold the bottom of the stretch which is often a recovered range of motion for another 5 seconds. Do 12-15 reps for 3 to 4 sets and obviously on both sides if they are both screwed up.

The seated spinal twist (Ardha Matsyendrasana variant) is a restorative yoga pose that improves spinal mobility, eases back/shoulder tension, and aids digestion. It involves sitting with legs extended, crossing one knee over, and twisting toward the top knee while keeping both sit bones grounded.
Inhale: Sit up tall, lengthening your spine toward the ceiling.
Exhale and Twist: Twist to the right, placing your right hand on the floor behind you for support.
Deepen the Twist: Hook your left elbow outside your right knee or hug the knee with your left arm.
